enrichment.short_description: Craft.io is an AI-powered, end-to-end product management platform that connects strategy, roadmapping, prioritization, and delivery for enterprise product teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See Craft.io's per-editor pricing, Guru AI features, and roadmapping tools in this deep dive to decide if the platform fits your product team's workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Craft.io is a product management platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel, with roughly 30 employees serving product teams across multiple continents. It positions itself as an end-to-end system for connecting product strategy, planning, and execution in a single collaborative workspace, aimed primarily at product managers, product leaders, and CPOs at mid-market and enterprise software companies. Core capabilities include roadmapping (timeline and Kanban-style views), backlog management, feature prioritization frameworks, capacity planning, and stakeholder alignment tools. A distinguishing feature is the 'Guru' layer, which embeds product management best practices directly into the workflow, paired with 'Guru AI,' an AI assistant that surfaces insights, automates repetitive tasks, and helps turn raw data into prioritization decisions. Craft.io also supports OKR management, letting teams link objectives and key results to initiatives, epics, and features, and build OKR-based roadmaps and dashboards. Enterprise customers get portfolio management across multiple products or business units, multi-level OKRs, automation rules, and advanced security controls including SSO, SAML, and audit logs. The platform integrates with common developer and design tools (such as Jira and other issue trackers) for two-way syncing between roadmap items and engineering tickets, plus dependency management to flag cross-team blockers. Pricing is structured around three tiers. Starter costs $24/month per editor billed monthly (or $19/month billed yearly) and includes a single workspace, limited views, and up to five custom fields, aimed at small teams getting started with structured roadmapping. Pro costs $99/month per editor monthly (or $79/month yearly) and unlocks unlimited workspaces, custom fields, dependency management, design-tool integrations, and optional paid add-ons for OKRs, a customer feedback portal, and capacity planning. Enterprise is custom-priced, billed annually, and bundles all Pro features and add-ons with portfolio-level roadmapping, advanced security, a dedicated customer success manager, and 24/7 support. Only 'editors' — admins and team leads who actively create or edit content — require a paid seat; contributors and viewers can be added at no additional cost, which differentiates Craft.io's pricing model from strictly per-seat competitors. A 14-day free trial is available on the Starter and Pro tiers with no credit card required. Craft.io is frequently compared to Productboard, Aha!, and Jira Product Discovery in the product management software category, and it markets itself specifically as distinct from note-taking or CMS tools with similar names. Given its per-editor pricing and enterprise security features, Craft.io tends to suit growing and established product organizations that need governance and portfolio visibility rather than solo builders or very early-stage startups.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Craft.io is a product management platform built to connect product strategy, roadmap planning, and day-to-day execution in one workspace. Founded in 2015 and based in Tel Aviv, Israel, the company focuses on giving product managers, product leaders, and executive stakeholders a shared source of truth for what is being built and why.

The platform is organized around roadmaps, a prioritized backlog, and OKRs, with a built-in 'Guru' layer that surfaces product management best practices as teams work, plus a Guru AI assistant that automates repetitive tasks like status summaries and prioritization scoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Roadmapping tools let teams build timeline and board-style roadmaps that can be filtered and shared with different stakeholder audiences, from engineering to the executive team.
OKR management connects company or team objectives directly to initiatives, epics, and features, so roadmap decisions can be traced back to strategic goals through dedicated OKR dashboards.
Prioritization and capacity planning tools apply scoring frameworks to the backlog and factor in team capacity, helping avoid overcommitment during planning cycles.

Dependency management flags cross-team or cross-feature blockers before they cause delivery slippage, and integrations with engineering tools like Jira keep roadmap items in sync with development tickets.
Enterprise-tier customers get portfolio management for coordinating multiple products or business units, along with SSO, SAML, and audit logging for governance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Craft.io prices by 'editor' seat rather than by every user: only people who actively create or modify roadmap content need a paid license, while contributors and viewers are unlimited and free.

Starter runs $24/month per editor (monthly) or $19/month per editor (billed yearly) and covers a single workspace with core roadmapping and backlog features. Pro runs $99/month per editor (monthly) or $79/month per editor (yearly) and adds unlimited workspaces, dependency management, and optional paid add-ons for OKRs, a feedback portal, and capacity planning.

Enterprise is custom-priced and billed annually, bundling every add-on with portfolio roadmaps, advanced security, and a dedicated customer success manager. A 14-day free trial is available on the lower tiers without requiring a credit card.

features.title: Strategic roadmapping
features.description: Build timeline and board-style roadmaps that link directly to OKRs and can be filtered for different stakeholder audiences.

features.title: Guru AI assistant
features.description: An AI layer that surfaces prioritization insights, automates status updates, and applies embedded product management best practices.

features.title: OKR management
features.description: Connect company and team objectives to initiatives, epics, and features with dedicated OKR dashboards for tracking progress.

features.title: Prioritization frameworks
features.description: Score and rank backlog items using built-in prioritization models to keep planning decisions consistent and defensible.

features.title: Capacity planning
features.description: Weigh team capacity against planned work to avoid overcommitting engineering resources during sprint or release planning.

features.title: Dependency management
features.description: Surface cross-team and cross-feature dependencies before they become delivery blockers.

features.title: Feedback portal
features.description: Collect and triage customer and stakeholder feedback, then link requests back to roadmap items for traceability.

features.title: Enterprise security controls
features.description: SSO, SAML, and audit logs on the Enterprise plan support governance requirements for larger organizations.

pros: Editor-based pricing means contributors and viewers can be added for free, lowering the cost of company-wide visibility
OKR-to-roadmap linkage gives leadership clear traceability from strategy to shipped features
Guru AI and the embedded best-practices layer help less experienced product managers apply structured frameworks
Strong enterprise security and portfolio management for organizations running multiple products
cons: Per-editor pricing can get expensive quickly for larger product teams, especially on the Pro tier
Several capabilities (OKRs, feedback portal, capacity planning) are gated as paid add-ons rather than included in Pro
Enterprise pricing is not published, requiring a sales conversation for larger deployments
Primarily built for structured, enterprise-style product management workflows, which can feel heavy for very small teams

faqs.question: Is Craft.io the same as the Craft notes app or Craft CMS?
faqs.answer: No. Craft.io is a dedicated product management platform for roadmapping and backlog prioritization. It is unrelated to the macOS/iOS note-taking app 'Craft' and unrelated to the open-source 'Craft CMS' content management system.

faqs.question: Does Craft.io offer a free plan?
faqs.answer: Craft.io does not offer a permanently free plan, but it provides a 14-day free trial on its Starter and Pro tiers without requiring a credit card.

faqs.question: How does Craft.io's pricing work?
faqs.answer: Craft.io charges per 'editor' seat — the people who create and modify roadmap content. Contributors and viewers, who only consume or comment on roadmaps, are unlimited and do not require a paid license.

faqs.question: What is Guru AI?
faqs.answer: Guru AI is Craft.io's built-in AI assistant that surfaces prioritization insights, automates routine tasks like status summaries, and applies embedded product management best practices as teams plan and build roadmaps.

faqs.question: Does Craft.io integrate with Jira?
faqs.answer: Yes, Craft.io integrates with common engineering issue trackers including Jira, syncing roadmap items with development tickets so product and engineering stay aligned.

use_cases.title: Enterprise roadmap alignment
use_cases.description: Product leaders use Craft.io to connect company OKRs to individual product roadmaps and give executives a shared, filterable view of priorities.
use_cases.audience: CPOs and VPs of Product at mid-market and enterprise companies

use_cases.title: Multi-product portfolio management
use_cases.description: Organizations running several products use the Enterprise tier's portfolio roadmaps to compare priorities and resourcing across business units.
use_cases.audience: Product operations teams at larger software companies

use_cases.title: Structured backlog prioritization
use_cases.description: Product managers apply built-in scoring frameworks and capacity planning to decide what to build next and defend those decisions to stakeholders.
use_cases.audience: Individual product managers and small product teams

features: Optional OKR, feedback portal, and capacity planning add-ons

enrichment.short_description: Craftable is a restaurant, bar, and hotel back-office platform that unifies purchasing, inventory management, recipe costing, and AP invoice automation.
enrichment.seo_title: Craftable Review: Restaurant Back-Office Software Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Craftable unifies purchasing, inventory counts, recipe costing, and AI-driven invoice automation for restaurants, bars, and hotels chasing tighter margins.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Craftable is a restaurant and hospitality back-office management platform founded in 2015, originally built around its Bevager (bar/beverage) and Foodager (food/purchasing) products before consolidating under the Craftable brand. The company is headquartered in Farmers Branch, Texas, with additional offices in San Mateo, California, and New York City, reflecting its blend of Silicon Valley engineering talent and hospitality industry operators. Craftable serves full-service and limited-service restaurants, bars, fast-casual concepts, and select- and full-service hotels and resorts, and states it is used by more than 10,000 operators, including named accounts such as bartaco, Jim's Steakout, and Kimpton Hotels. The core problem Craftable solves is replacing manual, spreadsheet-driven back-office processes with a connected system spanning purchasing, inventory, and accounts payable. Its 'Intelligent Ordering' module centralizes vendor order management, automatically flags price discrepancies across suppliers, and tracks purchase history in one place. The inventory and recipe management module ties physical stock counts to recipes and theoretical usage, so operators can spot margin loss — such as over-pouring or waste — before it shows up on financial statements. An AP automation module uses AI-assisted invoice processing to perform three-way matching between purchase orders, receiving records, and vendor invoices, flagging discrepancies and routing approvals to speed up the payables cycle. A unified dashboard combines food cost, labor, and purchasing data with AI-generated recommendations intended to surface daily, actionable operational decisions rather than end-of-month reports. Craftable reports operator outcomes including roughly a 5% reduction in cost of goods sold, a 50% reduction in time spent on inventory counts, and a 30% improvement in controlling unauthorized or 'rogue' spend outside approved vendor channels. Craftable does not publish standard pricing tiers; the company prices per location on a custom, quote-basis with unlimited user seats included, and each module (ordering, inventory, AP automation, analytics) can reportedly be purchased independently so operators only pay for what they use. There is no published free plan or free trial. Because pricing and onboarding are handled through direct sales conversations, Craftable is best suited to multi-location or growth-stage hospitality groups with dedicated back-office or finance staff, rather than single-location independent restaurants looking for a low-cost, self-serve tool. Competitors in this category include MarginEdge, xtraCHEF, and Sculpture Hospitality-style inventory consulting services, though Craftable differentiates itself with its combined purchasing-to-payables software stack rather than a single point solution.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Craftable is a back-office management platform for restaurants, bars, and hotels, built to replace spreadsheet-driven purchasing, inventory, and accounts payable processes with a connected, real-time system. Founded in 2015 and headquartered in Farmers Branch, Texas, the company grew out of its original Bevager (beverage) and Foodager (food) products, which are now offered together under the Craftable brand.

The platform targets multi-location hospitality operators — full- and limited-service restaurants, bars, fast-casual chains, and hotels — that need centralized visibility into food and beverage costs, vendor spend, and invoice processing across many sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Intelligent Ordering centralizes purchase orders across vendors, automatically catching price discrepancies and consolidating purchase history so operators can compare supplier pricing over time.

Inventory and recipe management links physical stock counts to standardized recipes and theoretical usage, helping identify variance and margin loss caused by waste, over-pouring, or portioning errors.
AP automation applies AI-assisted invoice processing with automatic three-way matching against purchase orders and receiving records, flagging mismatches and routing invoices for approval.
A unified analytics dashboard blends food cost, labor, and purchasing data with recommendations designed to support daily operational decisions rather than only month-end reporting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Craftable does not publish standard pricing tiers. The company prices per location on a custom quote basis, with unlimited user seats included in each contract.

Each core module — ordering, inventory, AP automation, and analytics — can reportedly be licensed independently, letting operators pay only for the pieces of the platform they need rather than a single bundled package.

features.title: Intelligent Ordering
features.description: Centralized vendor order management that automatically identifies price discrepancies and tracks purchase history across suppliers.

features.title: Inventory tracking
features.description: Real-time visibility into stock levels and costs, tied to recipes so variance and margin loss can be detected before they hit financial statements.

features.title: Recipe costing
features.description: Connects theoretical recipe usage to actual purchasing and inventory data to keep food and beverage costs aligned with menu pricing.

features.title: AP automation
features.description: AI-assisted invoice processing with automatic three-way matching between invoices, purchase orders, and receiving records.

features.title: Unified operations dashboard
features.description: Combines food cost, labor, and purchasing data into daily, actionable insights for operators and finance teams.

features.title: Multi-location support
features.description: Built for operators managing purchasing and inventory across many restaurant, bar, or hotel locations from one system.

pros: Combines purchasing, inventory, and accounts payable in one connected platform instead of separate point tools
AI-assisted invoice matching can meaningfully reduce manual AP workload for multi-location operators
Reported operator outcomes (reduced COGS, faster inventory counts) are grounded in real deployment data
Modular licensing lets operators start with the modules most relevant to their operation
cons: No public pricing, so evaluating cost requires a sales conversation and quote
No published free trial or free plan for smaller operators to test the platform
Best suited to multi-location or growth-stage operators, less compelling for single-unit independents
As a specialized hospitality back-office tool, it does not cover POS, scheduling, or front-of-house functions

faqs.question: What is Craftable used for?
faqs.answer: Craftable is back-office software for restaurants, bars, and hotels that centralizes purchasing, inventory management, recipe costing, and accounts payable invoice processing.

faqs.question: Is Craftable the same as Bevager or Foodager?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bevager and Foodager were Craftable's original beverage- and food-focused products; they are now offered together under the unified Craftable platform.

faqs.question: How much does Craftable cost?
faqs.answer: Craftable does not publish pricing. It charges per location on a custom quote basis, with unlimited user seats and modules (ordering, inventory, AP automation, analytics) that can be licensed independently.

faqs.question: Does Craftable offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Craftable does not advertise a public free trial or free plan; pricing and onboarding are handled through a direct sales process.

faqs.question: What kind of businesses use Craftable?
faqs.answer: Craftable is used by full-service and limited-service restaurants, bars, fast-casual concepts, and select- and full-service hotels, with named customers including bartaco, Jim's Steakout, and Kimpton Hotels.

use_cases.title: Multi-location inventory control
use_cases.description: Restaurant groups use Craftable to standardize inventory counts and recipe costing across locations, catching variance before it erodes margins.
use_cases.audience: Multi-unit restaurant and bar operators

use_cases.title: Accounts payable automation
use_cases.description: Finance teams use Craftable's AI-assisted invoice matching to reduce manual data entry and speed up vendor payment approvals.
use_cases.audience: Hospitality finance and accounting teams

use_cases.title: Centralized vendor purchasing
use_cases.description: Purchasing managers consolidate orders across suppliers and catch price discrepancies before they hit the bottom line.
use_cases.audience: Procurement and operations managers at hotels and restaurant groups

enrichment.short_description: Crafty Controller is a free, open-source web control panel for launching, managing, and monitoring Minecraft Java and Bedrock server instances.
enrichment.seo_title: Crafty Controller Review: Free Minecraft Server Panel
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Crafty Controller is a free, self-hosted web panel for running and managing Minecraft Java and Bedrock server instances from any browser or device.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crafty Controller is a free and open-source web-based control panel for launching, managing, and monitoring Minecraft server instances, distributed under the GNU General Public License v3. It is built with Python (3.10 or newer) and the Tornado web server, and its source code is publicly maintained on GitLab under the Crafty Controller project (with an earlier version's history on GitHub). The tool runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS, and offers pre-built Docker images and UnRAID templates for containerized or NAS-based deployments, along with a progressive web app for monitoring servers from a mobile browser. Crafty Controller supports any Minecraft server distribution that runs from a Java executable file — including vanilla, Paper, Spigot, Waterfall, CatServer, and modpack-based servers such as All The Mods — as well as Bedrock Edition server executables, making it useful for both Java and Bedrock server communities. Through its web interface, administrators can create and run multiple server instances, start and stop them independently, manage player lists and permissions, execute server console commands remotely, edit server configuration files and world files through a built-in file manager, and schedule recurring automated tasks such as backups and restarts. Security features include Argon2 password hashing for stored credentials, SSL/TLS certificate support for encrypting the web interface, and a fully self-hosted architecture, meaning all server data, configuration, and backups remain on the operator's own hardware rather than passing through a third-party cloud service. Because Crafty Controller is open source and self-hosted, there is no company behind it in the traditional SaaS sense, no pricing tiers, and no subscription plans — it is entirely free to download, install, and modify. The project is sustained by voluntary community donations through platforms like Ko-fi and Patreon rather than commercial licensing revenue. This makes Crafty Controller most comparable to other self-hosted game server management panels such as Pterodactyl or Multicraft, rather than to hosted Minecraft server providers. It is best suited for hobbyists, small communities, and system administrators who already have (or want) a server or VPS to host Minecraft on and prefer a web-based management layer over manually SSHing in and running server jar files by hand. Because it requires the user to provide and maintain their own server hardware or hosting, Crafty Controller is not a turnkey hosting service — it is purely the management and orchestration layer that sits on top of self-provided infrastructure. Its Docker and UnRAID support in particular have made it popular among home-lab and self-hosting communities that already run other containerized services.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Crafty Controller is a free, open-source, self-hosted web control panel for running Minecraft servers. Rather than manually starting a server jar file from a terminal, administrators use Crafty's browser-based interface to launch, stop, and monitor one or more Minecraft server instances from any device.

The project is licensed under the GNU General Public License v3, with its source code maintained publicly on GitLab, and it is sustained through voluntary community donations rather than a commercial pricing model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Crafty Controller supports any Minecraft server that runs from a Java executable file, including vanilla, Paper, Spigot, Waterfall, and modded distributions, as well as Bedrock Edition server executables.

The web interface provides server instance management, player and permission controls, remote console command execution, a built-in file manager for editing configuration and world files, and scheduled automated tasks such as backups.
It runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS, with pre-built Docker images and UnRAID templates for containerized deployments, plus a mobile-friendly progressive web app for monitoring servers on the go.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Crafty Controller is completely free to download, install, and modify, with no paid tiers or subscription plans. As open-source software, users provide and maintain their own server hardware or hosting; Crafty is purely the management layer on top of it.
The project accepts optional donations through Ko-fi and Patreon to support ongoing development, but no financial contribution is required to use any feature of the panel.

features.title: Multi-instance server management
features.description: Create, launch, stop, and monitor multiple Minecraft server instances independently from a single web dashboard.

features.title: Java and Bedrock support
features.description: Runs any Java-executable Minecraft server distribution (vanilla, Paper, Spigot, Waterfall, modpacks) as well as Bedrock Edition server executables.

features.title: Web-based file manager
features.description: Browse, edit, and upload server configuration files, plugins, and world data directly through the browser interface.

features.title: Remote console access
features.description: Send server commands and view live console output remotely without needing direct terminal or SSH access.

features.title: Scheduled automated tasks
features.description: Configure recurring scheduled tasks such as automatic backups and server restarts.

features.title: Docker and UnRAID support
features.description: Pre-built Docker images and UnRAID templates simplify containerized or NAS-based deployment.

features.title: Argon2 password hashing and SSL support
features.description: Secures the web panel with modern password hashing and optional SSL/TLS certificate encryption.

pros: Completely free and open source with no paid tiers or feature restrictions
Supports both Java and Bedrock Minecraft server executables in one panel
Self-hosted architecture keeps all server data and backups on the operator's own hardware
Docker and UnRAID support make it easy to fit into existing home-lab or self-hosting setups
cons: Requires users to provide and maintain their own server or hosting infrastructure; it is not a hosting service itself
As a community-maintained open-source project, support relies on documentation and community channels rather than paid customer support
Initial setup (Python environment, Docker, or manual install) requires more technical comfort than a fully managed hosting panel
No official native mobile apps beyond the browser-based progressive web app

faqs.question: Is Crafty Controller free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Crafty Controller is completely free and open source under the GNU General Public License v3. It is supported by optional community donations, not subscriptions.

faqs.question: Does Crafty Controller work with Bedrock Edition servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to any Java-executable Minecraft server (vanilla, Paper, Spigot, Waterfall, and modpacks), Crafty Controller supports Bedrock Edition server executables.

faqs.question: Do I need my own server to use Crafty Controller?
faqs.answer: Yes. Crafty Controller is a management panel, not a hosting provider — you need your own server, VPS, or home hardware to run Minecraft servers, and Crafty provides the web interface to manage them.

faqs.question: What operating systems does Crafty Controller support?
faqs.answer: Crafty Controller runs on Windows, Linux, and macOS, and also offers Docker images and UnRAID templates for containerized deployment.

faqs.question: Is Crafty Controller safe to expose to the internet?
faqs.answer: Crafty Controller includes Argon2 password hashing and supports SSL/TLS certificate encryption for the web panel, though as with any self-hosted admin tool, operators should follow standard security practices like firewalling and strong credentials.

use_cases.title: Home-lab Minecraft hosting
use_cases.description: Self-hosters run Crafty Controller on a home server or NAS via Docker to manage Minecraft servers for friends and family without manual command-line work.
use_cases.audience: Home-lab hobbyists and self-hosting enthusiasts

use_cases.title: Community server administration
use_cases.description: Server admins for larger Minecraft communities use Crafty's player management, scheduled backups, and remote console to operate servers without direct machine access.
use_cases.audience: Minecraft community and server administrators

use_cases.title: Multi-server instance management
use_cases.description: Operators running several Minecraft server instances (survival, creative, modded) on one machine use Crafty to manage them all from a single dashboard.
use_cases.audience: Small hosting providers and technical Minecraft server operators

enrichment.short_description: Creatio is a no-code CRM and business process management platform combining sales, marketing, and service automation with built-in AI agents.
enrichment.seo_title: Creatio Review 2026: No-Code CRM & BPM Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Creatio combines no-code CRM, marketing, service, and business process automation with built-in AI agents; see its 2026 pricing plans and features here.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Creatio is a no-code CRM and business process management (BPM) platform originally founded in 2002 in Ukraine as Terrasoft, which later rebranded its product line as 'bpm'online' before adopting the unified Creatio name in October 2019. The company is now headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, operates six offices with a presence in roughly 25 countries, employs more than 700 people, and has raised over $278 million in funding from investors including Volition Capital and Sapphire Ventures, reaching unicorn status (a $1.2 billion valuation) in 2024. Creatio's platform sits at the intersection of CRM and no-code process automation: it ships Sales, Marketing, and Service 'Creatio' modules built on top of a shared no-code development studio, so customers can both use out-of-the-box CRM functionality and visually build or customize their own business applications and workflows without writing code. The no-code studio includes a drag-and-drop process designer (BPMN-based), a visual data model builder, and Freedom UI page designers. Creatio.ai layers generative and predictive AI agents across the platform to draft content, score leads, summarize customer interactions, and recommend next-best actions inside sales, marketing, and service workflows. Pricing follows a per-user, per-month model with three core platform tiers: Growth at roughly $25/user/month for small and mid-sized teams starting with core no-code and CRM capabilities; Enterprise at roughly $55/user/month (some sources cite closer to $40-75/user/month depending on packaging) for larger organizations needing broader automation and integration; and Unlimited at roughly $85/user/month for organizations running complex, large-scale automation with no restrictions on use cases. On the Growth and Enterprise tiers, the Sales, Marketing, and Service Creatio modules are sold as separate add-ons at about $15/user/month each, while the Unlimited tier bundles all three modules at no extra per-user charge. Notably, Creatio enforces a minimum purchase commitment of roughly $10,000 per year, which makes it a poor fit for very small teams or solo users despite its per-user pricing structure, positioning it firmly as a mid-market-to-enterprise platform. Creatio is commonly evaluated against Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Pipedrive, and other no-code platforms like Zoho Creator, with its key differentiator being the tight integration between CRM functionality and a full no-code application development environment rather than CRM alone. It targets industries such as financial services, insurance, manufacturing, and professional services that need both standard CRM workflows and the flexibility to model custom, industry-specific business processes without a dedicated engineering team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Creatio is a no-code CRM and business process management platform with roots going back to 2002, when the company was founded as Terrasoft. The product line was rebranded to bpm'online and later unified under the Creatio name in 2019. Today the company is headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts, with more than 700 employees across six offices and a presence in around 25 countries.

The platform combines CRM functionality (Sales, Marketing, and Service modules) with a no-code development studio, so organizations can both run standard CRM workflows out of the box and build custom business applications and processes without writing code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The no-code studio includes a visual, BPMN-based process designer, a data model builder, and Freedom UI page designers, letting non-developers automate and customize business workflows.
Creatio.ai adds generative and predictive AI agents throughout the platform to draft outreach content, score and prioritize leads, summarize customer interactions, and recommend next-best actions.

Sales, Marketing, and Service Creatio modules cover pipeline management, marketing campaign automation, and case/ticket management respectively, all built on the same underlying no-code platform and data model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Creatio uses per-user, per-month pricing across three core tiers: Growth (around $25/user/month), Enterprise (around $55/user/month), and Unlimited (around $85/user/month), each unlocking progressively broader automation capabilities and fewer usage restrictions.

The Sales, Marketing, and Service Creatio modules are priced as add-ons of roughly $15/user/month each on the Growth and Enterprise tiers, while the Unlimited tier includes all three modules with no additional per-user module fee.
Creatio enforces a minimum annual purchase commitment of approximately $10,000, which effectively positions the platform for mid-market and enterprise buyers rather than very small teams.

features.title: No-code process designer
features.description: A visual, BPMN-based workflow builder lets non-developers automate and customize business processes without writing code.

features.title: Creatio.ai agents
features.description: Generative and predictive AI features draft content, score leads, summarize interactions, and recommend next-best actions across sales, marketing, and service.

features.title: Sales Creatio
features.description: Pipeline and opportunity management, forecasting, and sales process automation built on the shared no-code platform.

features.title: Marketing Creatio
features.description: Campaign management, lead nurturing, and marketing automation tools that share data and workflows with the CRM.

features.title: Service Creatio
features.description: Case and ticket management for customer service teams, with automated routing and SLA tracking.

features.title: Freedom UI page designer
features.description: Drag-and-drop page and application builder for creating custom business apps on top of the Creatio data model.

features.title: Unified data model
features.description: A shared, no-code-configurable data model underpins all CRM modules, keeping customer data consistent across sales, marketing, and service.

pros: Tight integration between CRM functionality and a genuine no-code development platform, not just CRM customization
Creatio.ai brings AI-assisted lead scoring, content drafting, and recommendations across the whole platform
Unlimited tier bundles all CRM modules, simplifying pricing for large deployments
Long track record (originally founded 2002) with substantial funding and enterprise customer base
cons: Minimum annual commitment of roughly $10,000 makes it inaccessible for very small teams or solo users
Sales, Marketing, and Service modules are separate paid add-ons on the lower tiers, adding to total cost
No-code process modeling has a learning curve for teams without prior BPM or workflow-automation experience
Pricing is comparatively complex, with per-user platform fees plus per-module fees depending on tier

faqs.question: Is Creatio the same company as bpm'online?
faqs.answer: Yes. Creatio was originally founded as Terrasoft in 2002, later rebranded its CRM/BPM product as bpm'online, and unified everything under the Creatio name in October 2019.

faqs.question: How much does Creatio cost?
faqs.answer: Creatio's core platform tiers start around $25/user/month (Growth) and scale to roughly $55/user/month (Enterprise) and $85/user/month (Unlimited), with a minimum annual purchase commitment of about $10,000.

faqs.question: Does Creatio require coding?
faqs.answer: No. Creatio is built as a no-code platform with a visual process designer and page builder, though it also supports custom development for teams that want to extend it with code.

faqs.question: What does Creatio.ai do?
faqs.answer: Creatio.ai is the platform's built-in AI layer, offering generative and predictive features such as lead scoring, content drafting, interaction summaries, and next-best-action recommendations.

faqs.question: Is Creatio suitable for small businesses?
faqs.answer: Creatio's roughly $10,000/year minimum commitment and per-module add-on pricing generally make it better suited to mid-market and enterprise organizations rather than very small businesses.

Sales, Marketing, Service modules available as add-ons

enrichment.short_description: Crisp is an AI-powered customer messaging platform that unifies live chat, email, and helpdesk conversations into one shared team inbox.
enrichment.seo_title: Crisp Review 2026: Live Chat & Helpdesk Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Crisp unifies live chat, email, and helpdesk conversations into one AI-powered shared inbox; compare its free plan and 2026 pricing tiers here.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Crisp is a customer messaging and support platform founded in 2015 by Baptiste Jamin and Valerian Saliou, headquartered in Nantes, France, with a small distributed team of roughly a few dozen employees. The product started as a website live chat widget and has expanded into a full omnichannel customer support suite that unifies live chat, shared team email inboxes, contact forms, and messaging channels into a single shared inbox, along with AI chatbot automation, a knowledge base builder, and a workflow automation tool. The chat widget itself supports automatic browser-based language detection and is localized in 60-plus languages, with a separate real-time message translation feature (branded LiveTranslate) that can translate conversations between agents and customers in 50-plus languages using a third-party translation API. Crisp's pricing is structured as a freemium, per-workspace subscription model rather than per-agent seat pricing, which differentiates it from many helpdesk competitors. The Free plan supports 2 agent seats and includes the core chat widget, a shared inbox, mobile apps, chat SDKs for web and mobile embedding, contact forms, e-commerce integrations, and push notifications, with no time limit. Paid tiers scale up from there: Mini at $45/month per workspace (4 seats) adds a shared email inbox, canned response shortcuts, chat triggers, private internal notes, a custom email domain, and unlimited data retention; Essentials at $95/month per workspace (10 seats) adds customer data management (a lightweight CRM), a true omnichannel inbox, a workflow automation builder, an omnichannel AI chatbot, a knowledge base, analytics, and routing/assignment rules; and Plus at $295/month per workspace (20-plus seats), marketed as the best-value tier, adds an AI-first support suite, unlimited automated workflows, a full ticketing system, 100-plus integrations, white-labeling, higher rate limits, and advanced analytics. Enterprise pricing is custom and includes dedicated onboarding, personalized SLAs, and team training. Each paid tier also includes a bundled allotment of AI credits (roughly $5 to $75 worth depending on tier, translating to 90 to 1,350 automated AI conversations per month) that power the chatbot and AI-assisted reply features, with additional usage billed beyond the included allotment. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial with full feature access. Crisp is commonly compared to Intercom, Zendesk, Tidio, and Freshdesk, and its main differentiators are its per-workspace (rather than strictly per-agent) pricing, a generous permanent free tier, and a unified inbox that blends live chat, email, and automation without requiring separate add-on products. It is used primarily by small and mid-sized e-commerce businesses, SaaS companies, and support teams that want an affordable, quick-to-deploy alternative to enterprise helpdesk suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Crisp is a customer messaging platform founded in 2015 by Baptiste Jamin and Valerian Saliou, headquartered in Nantes, France. What began as a website live chat widget has grown into a full customer support suite combining live chat, shared email, contact forms, AI chatbot automation, and a knowledge base into one shared team inbox.

The platform is built around a per-workspace pricing model rather than per-agent licensing, which lets teams add support agents without linearly increasing their subscription cost, distinguishing it from many competing helpdesk tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core widget offers real-time live chat with automatic browser-language detection and localization in 60-plus languages, plus LiveTranslate, a real-time conversation translation feature covering 50-plus languages.
The shared inbox unifies live chat, email, and other channels for support teams, with private internal notes, canned response shortcuts, and routing and assignment rules to distribute conversations.
AI features include an omnichannel AI chatbot and AI-assisted reply drafting, powered by a monthly AI credit allotment that scales with the pricing tier.
Additional tools include a workflow automation builder, a customer-facing knowledge base, a lightweight CRM for customer data, and a ticketing system on higher tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Crisp offers a permanent Free plan with 2 agent seats and core chat, inbox, and mobile app functionality. Paid plans are priced per workspace rather than per agent: Mini at $45/month (4 seats), Essentials at $95/month (10 seats), and Plus at $295/month (20-plus seats), each unlocking more automation, AI credits, and integrations.

Enterprise pricing is custom and adds dedicated onboarding, personalized SLAs, and team training. All paid plans include a 14-day free trial, and each tier bundles a monthly allotment of AI credits for chatbot and AI-reply features.

features.title: Shared team inbox
features.description: Unifies live chat, email, and other channels into one inbox so support agents never miss a conversation across channels.

features.title: AI chatbot and AI replies
features.description: An omnichannel AI chatbot and AI-assisted reply drafting, powered by a monthly AI credit allotment included in each paid tier.

features.title: LiveTranslate
features.description: Real-time conversation translation between agents and customers across 50-plus languages.

features.title: Knowledge base
features.description: A customer-facing help center builder for self-service articles, included from the Essentials tier up.

features.title: Workflow automation builder
features.description: Visual automation tool for triggering chat flows, routing rules, and follow-up actions based on customer behavior.

features.title: Chat widget and SDKs
features.description: Customizable website chat widget plus SDKs for embedding chat into web and mobile apps.

features.title: Customer data management
features.description: A lightweight CRM layer for storing and segmenting customer profiles alongside conversation history.

pros: Permanent free plan with real functionality (2 seats, shared inbox, mobile apps) rather than a short trial
Per-workspace pricing instead of per-agent seats can be significantly cheaper for larger support teams
Combines live chat, email, chatbot, and knowledge base without needing multiple separate tools
Widget supports 60-plus languages with real-time translation for genuinely global support
cons: AI features are metered by included credits, so heavy chatbot usage can require additional paid usage
Higher tiers (Essentials, Plus) get expensive quickly for teams that need omnichannel and automation features
Some advanced features like the full ticketing system and white-labeling are reserved for the top Plus tier
As a smaller, France-based company, its integration ecosystem is narrower than large incumbents like Zendesk or Intercom

faqs.question: Does Crisp have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. Crisp's Free plan includes 2 agent seats, the core chat widget, a shared inbox, mobile apps, and e-commerce integrations at no cost, with no time limit.

faqs.question: Is Crisp priced per agent or per workspace?
faqs.answer: Crisp is priced per workspace rather than strictly per agent. Each tier includes a set number of agent seats (for example, 10 on Essentials), so adding agents within that limit doesn't increase the price.

faqs.question: What languages does Crisp's chat widget support?
faqs.answer: The Crisp chat widget is localized in 60-plus languages with automatic browser-based language detection, and its LiveTranslate feature can translate live conversations across 50-plus languages.

faqs.question: Who founded Crisp and where is it based?
faqs.answer: Crisp was founded in 2015 by Baptiste Jamin and Valerian Saliou and is headquartered in Nantes, France.

faqs.question: Does Crisp offer a free trial on paid plans?
faqs.answer: Yes, all of Crisp's paid plans (Mini, Essentials, Plus) include a 14-day free trial with full feature access.

use_cases.title: E-commerce customer support

use_cases.audience: Small and mid-sized e-commerce businesses

use_cases.title: SaaS support and onboarding
use_cases.description: SaaS companies use Crisp's shared inbox, chatbot, and knowledge base to handle support tickets and guide new users through onboarding.
use_cases.audience: SaaS support and customer success teams

use_cases.title: Multilingual global support
use_cases.description: Support teams serving international customers use Crisp's widget localization and LiveTranslate to communicate across language barriers without hiring multilingual agents.
use_cases.audience: Global support teams and multinational businesses

enrichment.short_description: Cronicle is a free, open-source, self-hosted task scheduler with a web-based UI for running distributed, repeating jobs across worker servers.
enrichment.seo_title: Cronicle Review: Open-Source Task Scheduler Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cronicle is a free, self-hosted, open-source job scheduler with a web dashboard, live logs, and distributed worker server support for teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cronicle is a free, open-source task scheduler and job runner created by developer Joseph Huckaby, written in Node.js and released under the MIT license. It is designed as a modern, distributed replacement for traditional Unix cron, adding a web-based user interface, multi-server job distribution, and real-time monitoring on top of standard scheduled-job functionality. The project's source is maintained publicly on GitHub (jhuckaby/Cronicle) and its documentation site is hosted at cronicle.net. Cronicle handles three job types: scheduled recurring jobs (hourly, daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly, selected through a visual multi-select time picker rather than raw cron syntax), one-time on-demand jobs, and event-driven jobs. It supports distributing job execution across any number of worker servers, with automatic discovery of nearby Cronicle nodes on the network and automatic failover to backup servers if a primary node goes down, along with automatic retries for jobs that fail transiently. The web UI provides live, real-time log streaming for running jobs, graphical progress bars with estimated completion times, and historical performance graphs tracking each job's average CPU and memory usage over time, which helps operators spot performance regressions before they become outages. Notably, Cronicle requires no external database to operate — all job definitions, schedules, logs, and configuration are stored as JSON files on disk, which simplifies deployment and backup considerably compared to schedulers that require a separate database server. For integration with other systems, Cronicle supports API keys for authenticating external applications and custom webhooks that can notify outside systems (chat tools, incident management platforms, etc.) when jobs succeed, fail, or complete. Because Cronicle is a self-hosted open-source project rather than a commercial company, there is no pricing model, no vendor, and no company headquarters or employee count to report — it is free to download, deploy, and modify under the MIT license, and users are responsible for hosting it on their own servers or infrastructure. This makes Cronicle most directly comparable to other self-hosted job schedulers such as Rundeck, Apache Airflow (for more complex DAG-based workflows), or plain cron combined with a monitoring tool, rather than to hosted SaaS scheduling products like Cronitor. It is best suited for engineering and DevOps teams that want a lightweight, dependency-free, self-hosted alternative to cron with a real UI, multi-server support, and job history, without the operational overhead of running a full workflow orchestration platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cronicle is a free, open-source task scheduler and job runner created by developer Joseph Huckaby and released under the MIT license. Written in Node.js, it acts as a modern replacement for traditional Unix cron, adding a web-based dashboard, distributed job execution, and real-time monitoring.

The project requires no external database — job definitions, schedules, and logs are stored as JSON files on disk — which keeps deployment and backups simple compared to schedulers that depend on a separate database server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cronicle handles scheduled recurring jobs, one-time on-demand jobs, and event-driven jobs, with schedules configured through a visual multi-select time picker rather than raw cron syntax.
Jobs can be distributed across any number of worker servers, with automatic discovery of nearby nodes, automatic failover to backup servers, and automatic retries for transient failures.
The web dashboard provides live log streaming, graphical progress bars with estimated completion times, and historical CPU and memory usage graphs for each job.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cronicle is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, vendor, or subscription of any kind. Users self-host it on their own servers or infrastructure and are responsible for their own hosting costs.
There is no commercial company behind Cronicle; it is a community-maintained project accepting contributions through its public GitHub repository.

features.title: Visual job scheduling
features.description: Configure recurring jobs through a multi-select time picker for years, months, days, weekdays, hours, and minutes instead of raw cron syntax.

features.title: Distributed worker execution
features.description: Distribute job execution across any number of worker servers with automatic discovery and load balancing.

features.title: Automatic failover and retries
features.description: Jobs automatically fail over to backup servers and retry on transient failures without manual intervention.

features.title: Live log streaming
features.description: Real-time log viewer for running jobs, with graphical progress bars and estimated time remaining.

features.title: Historical performance graphs
features.description: Tracks average CPU and memory usage per job over time to help detect performance regressions early.

features.title: No database required
features.description: All job data, schedules, and logs are stored as JSON files on disk, simplifying deployment and backup.

features.title: API keys and webhooks
features.description: Authenticate external applications with API keys and trigger custom webhooks on job success, failure, or completion.

pros: Completely free and open source under the MIT license with no paid tiers
No external database dependency makes deployment and backups straightforward
Distributed, multi-server job execution with automatic failover goes well beyond plain cron
Real-time web UI with live logs and historical performance graphs improves visibility over standard cron
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing maintenance since there is no managed/hosted version
No dedicated commercial support; help comes from documentation and community channels
Less suited to complex, dependency-graph-based workflows compared to dedicated orchestration tools like Airflow
Smaller ecosystem and integration library than commercial scheduling and monitoring platforms

faqs.question: Is Cronicle free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cronicle is fully free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or subscription required.

faqs.question: Does Cronicle need a database?
faqs.answer: No. Cronicle stores all job definitions, schedules, and logs as JSON files on disk, so no external database server is required to run it.

faqs.question: Can Cronicle run jobs across multiple servers?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cronicle supports distributing jobs across any number of worker servers, with automatic discovery of nearby nodes and automatic failover to backups.

faqs.question: Who maintains Cronicle?
faqs.answer: Cronicle was created by developer Joseph Huckaby and is maintained as an open-source project on GitHub, with contributions from the community.

faqs.question: How is Cronicle different from regular cron?
faqs.answer: Unlike plain Unix cron, Cronicle adds a web-based UI, visual scheduling, multi-server job distribution with failover, live log streaming, and historical performance tracking.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted cron replacement
use_cases.description: DevOps teams replace scattered crontab entries across servers with a single Cronicle instance offering a web UI and centralized job history.
use_cases.audience: DevOps and infrastructure engineers

use_cases.title: Distributed batch job execution
use_cases.description: Teams use Cronicle to distribute recurring batch jobs (backups, reports, data syncs) across multiple worker servers with automatic failover.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams running scheduled batch workloads

use_cases.title: Job monitoring and history
use_cases.description: Teams rely on Cronicle's live logs and historical CPU/memory graphs to debug failed jobs and catch performance regressions before they cause outages.
use_cases.audience: Site reliability and platform engineering teams

enrichment.short_description: Cronitor is a developer-focused monitoring platform that tracks cron jobs, website uptime, and heartbeats, alerting teams the instant something fails.
enrichment.seo_title: Cronitor Review 2026: Cron & Uptime Monitoring Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cronitor monitors cron jobs, website uptime, and background processes, and alerts your team the moment something fails; see its 2026 pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cronitor is a monitoring platform for developers, founded in March 2014 by Shane Harter and a co-founder after one asked the other how they monitored their own cron jobs at work. The company is headquartered in Berkeley, California, and operates as a small, largely bootstrapped team, in contrast to many venture-funded monitoring competitors. Cronitor's core product captures the status, output, and execution metrics of scheduled jobs (cron jobs, Celery/Sidekiq-style background tasks, and other recurring scripts) and automatically alerts teams when a job fails, runs too long, or — critically — never starts at all, a failure mode that traditional logging and exception-tracking tools typically miss. Beyond cron job monitoring, the platform has expanded into general uptime monitoring for websites and APIs, heartbeat/check-in monitoring for any process that can ping an endpoint, and public or private status pages for communicating incidents to customers. The company states it is trusted by more than 50,000 developers and teams, with named customers including Johnson & Johnson, Reddit, Square, and monday.com. Cronitor's pricing follows a freemium, usage-based-per-monitor model. The free 'Hacker' plan includes 5 monitors, email and Slack alerting, a basic status page, and up to 50 status page subscribers, with no time limit. The paid 'Business' plan is pay-as-you-go at $2 per monitor per month plus $5 per user per month, and includes unlimited API requests, up to 10 alert integrations, 12 months of data retention, automated email reports, volume-based discounts as monitor count grows, support for multiple environments, SMS alerts, and premium integrations such as PagerDuty, Opsgenie, and Splunk; optional add-ons include SAML SSO ($5/user/month), branded status pages ($25/month), private status pages ($50/month), and additional status page subscriber capacity. The Business plan includes a 14-day free trial with no commitment required. Enterprise plans start from roughly $6,000 per year on flexible invoice billing and add custom monitoring limits, custom integrations, a dedicated engineer, priority support, and SAML SSO/SCIM access controls included at no extra cost. Cronitor is frequently compared to Healthchecks.io, Dead Man's Snitch, Better Uptime, and Datadog's monitor features, with its main differentiator being a purpose-built focus on scheduled-job and heartbeat monitoring rather than being a broad observability platform, making it a lighter-weight, faster-to-adopt option for engineering teams that primarily need to know when a recurring job silently stops running.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cronitor is a developer-focused monitoring platform founded in March 2014 by Shane Harter, headquartered in Berkeley, California. The product grew out of a simple problem: knowing when a cron job or scheduled background task silently fails or never starts, a failure mode traditional logging tools often miss.

Beyond cron job monitoring, Cronitor has expanded to cover website and API uptime monitoring, heartbeat/check-in monitoring for any recurring process, and status pages for communicating incident status publicly or privately.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cron and scheduled-job monitoring captures job status, execution duration, and output, alerting teams via email, Slack, SMS, PagerDuty, Opsgenie, or Splunk when a job fails, runs too long, or never starts.
Uptime monitoring checks websites and APIs on a regular interval and alerts on downtime or degraded response times, while heartbeat monitoring lets any process check in on an expected schedule.
Status pages (basic on the free plan, branded and private options on paid plans) let teams communicate incident status to internal or external stakeholders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cronitor's free Hacker plan includes 5 monitors, email and Slack alerts, and a basic status page. The Business plan is pay-as-you-go at $2 per monitor per month plus $5 per user per month, adding unlimited API requests, SMS alerts, premium integrations, and 12-month data retention, with a 14-day free trial.
Enterprise plans start from roughly $6,000 per year on flexible invoice billing, adding custom limits, a dedicated engineer, priority support, and SAML SSO/SCIM at no extra cost.

features.title: Cron job monitoring
features.description: Tracks execution status, duration, and output of cron jobs and scheduled scripts, alerting on failures, timeouts, or missed runs.

features.title: Heartbeat monitoring
features.description: Lets any process or background job check in on an expected schedule, flagging when a check-in is missed.

features.title: Uptime monitoring
features.description: Regularly checks websites and APIs and alerts the team on downtime or degraded performance.

features.title: Multi-channel alerting
features.description: Sends alerts via email, Slack, SMS, PagerDuty, Opsgenie, and Splunk depending on plan tier.

features.title: Status pages
features.description: Basic status pages on the free plan, with branded and private status page options available on paid plans.

features.title: Data retention and reporting
features.description: 12 months of monitoring data retention with automated email reports on the Business plan.

pros: Purpose-built for cron and scheduled-job monitoring, catching failure modes like jobs that never start
Generous free Hacker plan with 5 monitors and Slack/email alerting at no cost
Transparent, pay-as-you-go pricing per monitor rather than opaque bundled tiers
Trusted by large organizations (Johnson & Johnson, Reddit, Square, monday.com) despite being a small team
cons: Small team size may mean slower feature velocity compared to larger, venture-funded monitoring platforms
Per-monitor and per-user pricing can add up quickly for teams monitoring many jobs and services
Premium integrations (PagerDuty, Opsgenie, Splunk) and SMS alerts require the paid Business tier
Enterprise pricing starting at $6,000/year is a significant jump from the self-serve Business plan

faqs.question: What does Cronitor monitor?
faqs.answer: Cronitor monitors cron jobs and scheduled tasks, website and API uptime, and heartbeat/check-in signals from any recurring process, alerting teams when something fails, runs too long, or never starts.

faqs.question: Does Cronitor have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cronitor's free Hacker plan includes 5 monitors with email and Slack alerting and a basic status page, with no time limit.

faqs.question: How is Cronitor priced?
faqs.answer: Cronitor's Business plan is pay-as-you-go at $2 per monitor per month plus $5 per user per month. Enterprise plans start from roughly $6,000 per year with custom limits and a dedicated engineer.

faqs.question: Who founded Cronitor?
faqs.answer: Cronitor was founded in March 2014 by Shane Harter, and the company is headquartered in Berkeley, California.

faqs.question: Does Cronitor support SMS or PagerDuty alerts?
faqs.answer: SMS alerts and premium integrations like PagerDuty, Opsgenie, and Splunk are included on the paid Business plan; the free Hacker plan is limited to email and Slack alerts.

use_cases.title: Cron job failure detection
use_cases.description: Engineering teams monitor critical cron jobs and background tasks so they're alerted immediately if a job fails or silently stops running.
use_cases.audience: Backend and DevOps engineers

use_cases.title: Website and API uptime tracking
use_cases.description: Teams monitor production websites and APIs for downtime or slow response times, with status pages to keep stakeholders informed during incidents.
use_cases.audience: Site reliability engineers and infrastructure teams

use_cases.title: Heartbeat monitoring for data pipelines
use_cases.description: Data engineering teams use heartbeat checks to confirm that ETL jobs and data pipelines are running on schedule.
use_cases.audience: Data engineering teams

enrichment.short_description: Cronofy is a calendar API and embeddable scheduling platform that syncs Google, Outlook, and Apple calendars for developers and recruiting teams.
enrichment.seo_title: Cronofy Review 2026: Calendar API & Scheduling Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cronofy provides a calendar sync API and embeddable scheduling tools for Google, Outlook, and Apple Calendar; compare its 2026 pricing plans here.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cronofy is a calendar synchronization API and scheduling platform founded in 2014 by engineers Adam Bird and Garry Shutler, headquartered in Nottingham, United Kingdom, with additional offices in London and Amsterdam and roughly 46 employees. The company builds infrastructure for developers and product teams who need reliable, real-time calendar connectivity across Google Calendar, Microsoft 365/Outlook, Apple Calendar, and Exchange without building and maintaining that integration layer themselves. Cronofy's product spans two main surfaces: a Scheduler (embeddable scheduling links and widgets for booking meetings, similar in end-user experience to Calendly) and a developer-facing Calendar API/SDK for building fully custom scheduling and availability experiences inside another product, such as an applicant tracking system or telehealth platform. The company reports its infrastructure has been used to schedule hundreds of millions of events and is particularly well known in the HR technology and recruiting space, where it powers interview-scheduling features for applicant tracking systems, alongside customers in healthcare, financial services, and enterprise software. Key capabilities include real-time two-way calendar sync (so busy/free status and new bookings update instantly across a candidate's or employee's actual calendar), availability rule sets, group and round-robin scheduling for panel interviews, browser extensions for Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Outlook, and native integrations with recruiting and HR tools such as Greenhouse, Lever, SmartRecruiters, Zoom, Zendesk, HubSpot, and Okta. Cronofy's pricing splits into Scheduler plans and API plans, both using a 'pay for active users, not empty seats' dynamic billing model, meaning seats are assigned based on actual monthly usage rather than total provisioned accounts. Scheduler pricing starts with a Team plan at $15 per active seat per month (unlimited calendar connections and bookings, multi-user scheduling, browser extensions), scaling to a Business plan at $799 per month billed annually (covering up to 50 active seats, adding Scheduler API access, custom domain/branding, and recruiting-tool integrations like Greenhouse and SmartRecruiters), with a custom-priced Enterprise tier above that at roughly $2,999/month equivalent for larger deployments. Separately, API-only pricing for developers building custom integrations starts at a Starter tier around $139/month, scaling through Emerging (~$819/month) and Growth (~$2,399/month) tiers based on usage volume, with a custom Strategic tier for the largest integrators. A minimum monthly commitment of $99 applies to standalone use of Cronofy's AI-driven Meeting Agents feature. A free trial is available across the product line. Cronofy is most commonly compared to Calendly, Nylas, and Google/Microsoft's native calendar APIs, with its core differentiator being deep specialization in reliable, real-time, multi-provider calendar sync as developer infrastructure rather than a general-purpose scheduling app alone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cronofy is a calendar synchronization API and scheduling platform founded in 2014 by engineers Adam Bird and Garry Shutler, headquartered in Nottingham, UK, with additional offices in London and Amsterdam. It provides the calendar infrastructure that other products build scheduling features on top of, connecting to Google Calendar, Microsoft 365/Outlook, Apple Calendar, and Exchange.

The company serves two audiences: end users who book meetings through Cronofy's embeddable Scheduler links, and developers who integrate Cronofy's Calendar API/SDK directly into their own product, such as an applicant tracking system or telehealth app that needs real-time availability and booking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Real-time, two-way calendar sync keeps busy/free status and new bookings accurate across a user's actual calendar the moment they change, rather than relying on periodic polling.
Group and round-robin scheduling supports panel interviews and team-based booking, while configurable availability rules let organizations define working hours, buffers, and booking limits.
Native integrations with recruiting and HR tools such as Greenhouse, Lever, SmartRecruiters, Zoom, Zendesk, HubSpot, and Okta make Cronofy especially popular for interview scheduling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cronofy's Scheduler plans start at $15 per active seat per month (Team), scaling to $799/month billed annually for Business (up to 50 active seats plus Scheduler API access), with a custom Enterprise tier above that. Billing follows a dynamic 'pay for active users' model based on actual monthly usage rather than provisioned seats.

Separately, API-only pricing for developers starts around $139/month (Starter) and scales through Emerging (~$819/month) and Growth (~$2,399/month) tiers based on usage volume, with a custom Strategic tier for the largest integrators. A free trial is available.

features.title: Real-time calendar sync
features.description: Two-way synchronization with Google Calendar, Microsoft 365/Outlook, Apple Calendar, and Exchange that updates instantly as events change.

features.title: Embeddable Scheduler
features.description: Booking links and widgets that let external users schedule meetings directly into a host's connected calendar.

features.title: Calendar API and SDKs
features.description: Developer-facing APIs for building fully custom scheduling and availability experiences inside another product.

features.title: Group and round-robin scheduling
features.description: Supports panel interviews and team-based booking by coordinating availability across multiple calendars at once.

features.title: Recruiting tool integrations
features.description: Native connections to Greenhouse, Lever, SmartRecruiters, and other ATS platforms for interview scheduling workflows.

features.title: Browser extensions
features.description: Extensions for Chrome, Edge, Firefox, and Outlook that bring scheduling links directly into email and browsing workflows.

pros: Deep specialization in real-time, multi-provider calendar sync rather than a bolt-on feature of a broader product
Strong presence in HR tech and recruiting through integrations with major ATS platforms like Greenhouse and Lever
Dynamic 'active user' billing avoids paying for provisioned-but-unused seats
Separate API-only pricing lets pure developer integrators pay based on usage rather than per-seat Scheduler pricing
cons: Business and Enterprise Scheduler tiers ($799/month and up) are significantly pricier than consumer scheduling tools like Calendly
API pricing tiers scale quickly with usage volume, which can be hard to predict for growing integrations
A $99/month minimum commitment applies to standalone use of the Meeting Agents AI feature
Primarily positioned for B2B and developer use cases, offering less value for individual solo schedulers

faqs.question: What is Cronofy used for?
faqs.answer: Cronofy provides calendar synchronization and scheduling infrastructure, used both directly as an embeddable Scheduler for booking meetings and as a Calendar API for developers building custom scheduling features into their own products.

faqs.question: Which calendars does Cronofy support?
faqs.answer: Cronofy connects with Google Calendar, Microsoft 365/Outlook, Apple Calendar, and Exchange, syncing availability and bookings in real time.

faqs.question: How much does Cronofy cost?
faqs.answer: Cronofy's Scheduler plans start at $15 per active seat per month (Team) up to $799/month (Business, annual billing). Separate API-only pricing starts around $139/month for developers building custom integrations.

faqs.question: Who founded Cronofy?
faqs.answer: Cronofy was founded in 2014 by engineers Adam Bird and Garry Shutler and is headquartered in Nottingham, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Is Cronofy popular for recruiting and interview scheduling?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cronofy is widely used in HR technology, with native integrations into applicant tracking systems like Greenhouse, Lever, and SmartRecruiters for coordinating interview scheduling.

use_cases.title: Interview scheduling for recruiting teams
use_cases.description: Talent acquisition teams use Cronofy's integrations with Greenhouse, Lever, and SmartRecruiters to coordinate panel interviews across multiple interviewer calendars.
use_cases.audience: Recruiting and talent acquisition teams

use_cases.title: Embedded scheduling in SaaS products
use_cases.description: Developers use Cronofy's Calendar API to build native scheduling and availability features directly into their own product, such as a telehealth or services marketplace app.
use_cases.audience: Product engineering teams at SaaS companies

use_cases.title: Meeting booking links
use_cases.description: Sales and customer success teams use Cronofy's embeddable Scheduler links to let prospects and customers book meetings without back-and-forth emails.
use_cases.audience: Sales and customer success teams
